A Stitch in Time Saves Nine

If only the ancients who wrote these texts could imagine how difficult it would be to train computers to translate their work! Working with data from 1950-1750 BCE is not for the faint of heart. From 1927 CE until today, modern scholars have been working with the editing and publication of these ca. 8,000 documents in many different languages, and despite (or because of) almost 100 years of scholarly scrutiny, the datasets are more complicated and in a more convoluted format, than what the ancients originally wrote. These editions were migrated into a database, which became the foundation of the data we used for the training and supplemental data for this challenge. Unbeknownst to them, we are now re-purposing their editions for training ML models, and the messiness of the data is palpable for all to see. That said, this is the first benchmark for a significant ML application with the Old Assyrian data, so with great struggle comes greater pioneering outcomes.

The purpose of this final update is to level the number of markers for gaps from two to one. The hope is that by making some small, but significant adjustments, this will improve the scores for everyone. What we refrained from doing was making any additional changes which could introduce new inconsistencies if not done uniformly. So for the final month of the competition, we will include a clear and concise list of recommendations for the training dataset for alignment with the test data.

Dataset Update - Mind the Gaps

Hi everyone,

I just posted an update to the dataset that regularizes <gap> conventions. The train.csv, published.csv, the (hidden) test.csv, and the test set labels have all been updated. The competition host will follow up soon with more details.

I will also be initiating a rescore of all current submissions tomorrow. I will keep you updated as this progresses. All new submissions will be scored against the updated labels.

UPDATE 02/20/2026: We are working on another (hopefully final) update that should address some of the issues raised here. I hope to have it out soon, likely Monday. We will delay the rescore until then.

arrow_drop_up

more_vert
Two practical stumbling blocks in Akkadian → English MT (and how to address them)

Based on recent experiments and discussions with several participants, I want to share a set of observations about what appear to be the primary bottlenecks in this competition. These are not model-architecture issues per se, but data- and representation-level issues that strongly affect downstream training, evaluation, and reinforcement learning.

After multiple iterations, debugging sessions, and failed-but-informative experiments, two main stumbling blocks consistently emerge:

(1) Named entities (personal names, place names, divine names) (2) Inconsistent ASCII / transliteration formats across Akkadian datasets

Both issues disproportionately affect tokenization, alignment, and reward stability, and in practice they limit performance more than model size or optimizer choice.

1. Named entities are a dominant source of error

Personal names, geographic names, and divine names behave very differently from ordinary lexical items:

They are often transliterated inconsistently across editions.
They frequently preserve older orthographic conventions.
They are semantically opaque to the model unless explicitly supported.

In experiments, many otherwise reasonable translations fail almost entirely because a name is mangled, dropped, or hallucinated. This affects not only final accuracy but also reward-based methods, where small orthographic deviations can collapse a sentence-level score.

To addr

Welcome to the Deep Past Initiative Machine Translation Challenge!

We’re excited to have you here on Kaggle for the launch of the Deep Past Initiative Machine Translation Challenge. This competition invites you to work with one of the world’s oldest written languages and to explore how modern machine translation can be applied to historically and philologically complex data.

Our top priority is to make sure you have the tools, context, and community support needed to succeed.

Getting Started

Before diving in, please take time to carefully review the Data tab, especially the section on the Suggested Workflow for Building Additional Training Data, which outlines recommended strategies and resources for improving model performance.

Connect with the Community & Stay Updated

Plug into the broader Deep Past Initiative ecosystem:

Website: https://www.deeppast.org

Discord: https://discord.gg/XtCMyTrVCF

X / Twitter: @DeepPastAI

LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/company/deep-past-initiative/

These channels are where we share announcements, clarifications, dataset updates, and broader discussions about ancient language NLP.

Leaderboard & Open-Source Requirement

This competition uses a public/private leaderboard. In keeping with the spirit of open research and reproducibility, participants eligible for prizes must open-source their final workflows. Failure to do so will result in removal from prize eligibility.

Questions?

Feel free to:

Start a Discussion thread here on

Old Assyrian dataset updates

Here are the links to some datasets I gathered together:

1) https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/deeppast/old-assyrian-grammars-and-other-resources/data The first link includes some good Old Assyrian grammars, zips of Akkadian lexicons, and the much anticipated onomasticon.csv along with some other PDFs that should be helpful for those who know how to work with PDFs or OCR output.

Note that the onomasticon.csv is not entirely complete. If one were to use a matching search on the written forms in this list in comparison with the OA_Lexicon_eBL.csv, I am sure there would be a lot of "aliases" found. Because I joined together a lot of these lists of names (name catalogs) I didn't finish deduplicating everything. So by all means, please help with the final version of the onomasticon. But I think all the elements are there to help with the spellings of the majority of the names in any case, including those in the test set.

2) https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/deeppast/old-assyrian-kltepe-tablets-in-pdf/data The second link includes the major Old Assyrian text editions in PDF format for this dataset. While these volumes are included in the publications.csv, the outcome of the OCR was less than ideal. So perhaps sharing the PDFs will allow for a better outcome of the texts which are in alignment in these volumes. While this is not everything in the .csv file, it should help with getting the formatting of the texts as well, since the test set is also simil

Data Update

Hi everyone,

We just published several new supplemental data files. We'll try to add more comprehensive documentation for these in the coming days, but for now:

eBL_Dictionary.csv The complete dictionary of Akkadian words from the eBL database. It collects the data provided by the URLs at eBL in the OA_Lexicon_eBL.csv file. (No need to scrape the website now!)
resources.csv A list of resources that might be used for additional data.
Sentences_Oare_FirstWord_LinNum.csv An aid to aligning translations at the sentence level for the data in train.csv. Indicates the first word of each sentence and its location on the tablet.

We hope you find these useful!

25

https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/deep-past-initiative-machine-translation/overview/dataset-instructions

For deity / divinity, it reports it should be preceded by {d} but it seems to (d) instead?

{d} = dingir ‘god, deity’ — d preceding non-human divine actors

For instance: 004a7dbd-57ce-46f8-9691-409be61c676e

KIŠIB ma-nu-ba-lúm-a-šur DUMU ṣí-lá-(d)IM KIŠIB šu-(d)EN.LÍL DUMU ma-nu-ki-a-šur KIŠIB MAN-a-šur DUMU a-ta-a
Seal of Mannum-balum-Aššur son of Ṣilli-Adad, seal of Šu-Illil son of Mannum-kī-Aššur, seal of Puzur-Aššur son of Ataya.

How to get started + Competition's Official Discord
Information for newbies

New to machine learning and data science? No question is too basic or too simple. Feel free to start your own thread, or use this thread as a place to post any first-timer clarifying questions for the Kaggle community to help you with!

New to Kaggle? Take a look at a few videos to learn a bit more about site etiquette, Kaggle lingo, and how to enter a competition using Kaggle Notebooks. Publish and share your models on Kaggle Models!

Looking for a team? Express your interest in joining a team through our Team Up feature.

Remember: Kaggle is for everyone. Whether you're teaming up or sharing tips in the competition forum, we expect everyone to follow our Kaggle community guidelines.

Competition's Official Discord

In addition to this competition forum, you can continue the discussion in our official Kaggle Discord Server here:

discord.gg/kaggle

The Discord is a great place to ask getting started questions, chat about the nuances of this competition, and connect with potential team mates. Learn more about Discord at our announcement here. Here are a few things to keep in mind though:

Yes, and it has really helped to burn me out faster. We were in the lead for the Vesuvius comp. Tested hundreds of experiments locally and then with 4 days remaining they change the data and add two weeks. Still happy with the result and more importantly I learned a lot but that would have been really cool for us. On top of that I know the prize would be equivalent to many months of salary for some of my teammates.

I understand wishing you did things differently and even adjusting things in early comp to correct for mistakes. But for the sake of competition, it just seems like bad practice that reduces how great the platform is. If it's possible you would think to take the extra few weeks to get the data and the metric right.

Massive bot attack

Today I noticed an unusual spike in the number of teams entering the competition. I took a look at the public LB and found out that more than 140 solo participants joined the competition and submitted the same public notebook. It is usually 15-20 teams a day for the last 2-3 weeks. Places 1272–1412 are now occupied by freshly created contributor accounts with no history.

Does anyone know what it is? Are these bots? Or did some institution ask students to participate? Nicknames are diverse and submission intervals are even 4 to 5 minutes — a clear uniform distribution! I do think these are bots. I have never seen anything like this.

@ryanholbrook can you please take a look. It looks very suspicious

EDIT: I took my investigation further and manualy checked arbitrary 10 accounts. They ALL joined exactly DPC, Stanford RNA 3D Folding Part 2 and AIMO. And ALL of them made exactly 1 submission

EDIT2: Now these accounts submitted more notebooks. Most of them now have 3 submissions. And now they do not occupy 140+ consecutive places and it's not easy to detect all of them in few minutes.

Please sign in to reply to this topic.

#cleaned words from eBL_Dictionary

Insights from the Akkademia Codebase & PNAS Paper for the Deep Past Challenge

insights from https://github.com/gaigutherz/Akkademia/blob/master/akkadian/ -> https://claude.ai/chat/ae982bc5-0d2e-41ea-a35c-c5e5f2dfccf3

Insights from the Akkademia Codebase & PNAS Paper for the Deep Past Challenge Here's what you can extract and apply from the Akkademia project:

1. Architecture & Models

Akkademia uses HMM, MEMM and BiLSTM neural networks for transliteration, achieving accuracy rates of 89.5% with HMM, 94% with MEMM, and 96.7% with BiLSTM on the trained corpora. GitHub But the translation work (the 2023 PNAS Nexus paper) used something different — a Fairseq convolutional model (fconv) for the NMT task, as CNNs have been shown to work well for low-resource and character-level machine translation, with shorter training time compared with transformers. PubMed Central

Key takeaway for you: Their SOTA results used CNNs, not transformers. This is a 2023 result. Modern transformers (especially ByT5, mT5, or NLLB) with proper fine-tuning should be able to beat 37.47 BLEU4. The competition is essentially asking you to outperform this benchmark on a different (Old Assyrian) corpus.

2. Two Translation Pipelines — and Why C2E ≈ T2E

The repo supports two tasks:

C2E (Cuneiform Unicode → English): Character-based tokenization with a small vocabulary of ~400 characters
T2E (Transliteration → English): BPE tokenization via SentencePiece — 1,000 vocab for transliteration, 10,000

Question about Score Discrepancies

Hi everyone—thank you for running this competition. I have a quick question about scoring. I recently joined this competition and noticed that the following three scores do not match:

・the score shown in the list on the competition page’s Code tab

・the score shown on the notebook’s individual page

・the score I actually receive when I submit the notebook

Is this discrepancy due to a dataset update from a few weeks ago, or is it expected behavior specific to this competition? I’m seeing a similar situation in the currently running Stanford RNA competition as well.

If this has already been answered elsewhere, I’d be grateful if you could point me to the relevant discussion. Thank you!

Is this competition becoming a 'Regex Guessing Game'?

I recently noticed this discussion about the dataset update. While I am genuinely grateful to the Kaggle staff and the host for their continuous efforts to improve the data quality, I must admit I am starting to lose motivation and feel a bit exhausted by this process… Even after the recent updates, the dataset still lacks a fully unified and consistent standard.

I joined this competition to focus my energy on trying new LLM/Seq2Seq strategies, experimenting with advanced NMT training methods, and learning meaningful domain knowledge from both the host and other competitors. Instead, I find myself spending massive amounts of time reading discussion threads just to bridge the information gap, and writing endless Regex pipelines to guess the correct data format.

GPU for Local Training

Hello, I have a question I'm curious about. Since I usually participate in playgrounds or competitions held in my country, I haven't had much opportunity to use GPUs other than the one Kaggle provides us. I also want to participate in global competitions, but while the Kaggle GPU is sufficient for inference, I'm not sure if Kaggle GPUs are enough for training. Or generally, I'm not sure if Kaggle is used for training in global competitions. If the Kaggle GPU is not enough, could you suggest a good GPU that I would need to participate in competitions?

Please sign in to reply to this topic.

comment
2 Comments
Hotness
xavier fernando

Posted 3 days ago

· 850th in this Competition

arrow_drop_up
1
more_vert

For this competition, Kaggle is sufficient for both training and inference—you can refer to the public notebooks for reference. If you plan to use a rental GPU, you can choose one based on your requirements. This mainly depends on the competition dataset and the batch size you intend to use. In most cases, an RTX 3090 or RTX 4090 with 24 GB of GPU memory is more than adequate.
